# title: Find the maximum number in a list using Stream API.
answer (Markdown): Use `numbers.stream().max(Integer::compareTo)`; it returns an `Optional<Integer>`. Time: O(n). Space: O(1). Handle an empty list with `orElse`, `orElseThrow`, or by returning the Optional.

# title: Find the minimum number in a list using Stream API.
answer (Markdown): Use `numbers.stream().min(Integer::compareTo)`; it returns an `Optional<Integer>`. Time: O(n). Space: O(1). Decide explicitly how an empty list should be handled.

# title: Count strings that start with a specific letter using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `strings.stream().filter(s -> s.startsWith(prefix)).count()`. The result is a `long`. Time: O(n) string checks. Filter null elements first if the input may contain nulls.

# title: Convert a list of strings to uppercase using Stream API.
answer (Markdown): Use `strings.stream().map(s -> s.toUpperCase(Locale.ROOT)).toList()`. Time: O(total characters). Use `Locale.ROOT` for locale-independent results and decide how nulls should be treated.

# title: Find the sum of all even numbers in a list using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `numbers.stream().filter(n -> n % 2 == 0).mapToInt(Integer::intValue).sum()`. Time: O(n). Space: O(1), excluding the source list. An empty stream sums to zero.

# title: Remove duplicate elements from a list using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `values.stream().distinct().toList()`. Encounter order is preserved for ordered streams. Time is typically O(n), with O(n) additional space for seen values. Correct equality depends on `equals` and `hashCode`.

# title: Sort a list of integers in ascending order using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `numbers.stream().sorted().toList()`. Time: O(n log n). Space: O(n). The source list is not modified; null values require filtering or a null-aware comparator.

# title: Sort a list of integers in descending order using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `numbers.stream().sorted(Comparator.reverseOrder()).toList()`. Time: O(n log n). Space: O(n). Filter nulls or provide a null-aware comparator when needed.

# title: Find the first element of a list using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `values.stream().findFirst()`, which returns an `Optional<T>`. It respects encounter order, including on parallel ordered streams. Time is O(1) for a sequential list stream in the usual case.

# title: Find any element of a list using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `values.stream().findAny()`. It returns an `Optional<T>` and may select any element, which allows better parallel execution. Do not rely on it returning the first element.

# title: Check whether a list contains a specific element using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `values.stream().anyMatch(target::equals)`. It short-circuits after a match. Time: O(n) worst case; space: O(1). Use `Objects.equals(value, target)` when nulls are possible.

# title: Count occurrences of each word using groupingBy.
answer (Markdown): Use `words.stream().collect(Collectors.groupingBy(Function.identity(), Collectors.counting()))`. Time: O(n). Space: O(k), where k is the number of distinct words. Normalize case first if counting should be case-insensitive.

# title: Find the longest string in a list using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `strings.stream().max(Comparator.comparingInt(String::length))`. It returns an Optional. Time: O(n). For equal lengths, this keeps one matching value; filter nulls before measuring length.

# title: Find the shortest string in a list using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `strings.stream().min(Comparator.comparingInt(String::length))`. It returns an Optional. Time: O(n). Define whether empty strings are valid and filter nulls if necessary.

# title: Partition numbers into even and odd using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `numbers.stream().collect(Collectors.partitioningBy(n -> n % 2 == 0))`. The result is a `Map<Boolean,List<Integer>>`; true contains evens. Time: O(n). Space: O(n).

# title: Convert integers into a comma-separated string using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `numbers.stream().map(String::valueOf).collect(Collectors.joining(","))`. Time and space are O(total output length). An empty stream produces an empty string.

# title: Find duplicate elements in a list using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use a frequency map, then retain entries whose count is greater than one: `groupingBy(identity(), counting())`. This avoids unsafe mutation inside the stream. Time: O(n). Space: O(k).

# title: Flatten a list of lists into a single list using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `nested.stream().flatMap(Collection::stream).toList()`. Time and output space are O(m), where m is the total number of elements. Filter null inner lists if the input permits them.

# title: Find the second highest number in a list using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `numbers.stream().distinct().sorted(Comparator.reverseOrder()).skip(1).findFirst()`. Time: O(n log n); space: O(n). `distinct()` means second highest distinct value; omit it if duplicates count as positions.

# title: Find the second lowest number in a list using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `numbers.stream().distinct().sorted().skip(1).findFirst()`. Time: O(n log n); space: O(n). The result is empty when fewer than two distinct values exist.

# title: Find common elements between two lists using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Create a `HashSet` from the second list, then use `first.stream().filter(secondSet::contains).distinct().toList()`. Expected time: O(n+m). Space: O(m). Define whether duplicate results should be retained.

# title: Merge two lists into one using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `Stream.concat(first.stream(), second.stream()).toList()`. Time and output space: O(n+m). The resulting list is unmodifiable with `Stream.toList()`; collect into an `ArrayList` if mutation is required.

# title: Find distinct characters in a string using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `text.codePoints().distinct().mapToObj(Character::toString).toList()`. Code points handle supplementary Unicode characters better than `chars()`. Time: O(n); space: O(k).

# title: Reverse a list using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Streams have no reverse operation. Use `IntStream.range(0, list.size()).mapToObj(i -> list.get(list.size()-1-i)).toList()` for a random-access list. Time: O(n) for ArrayList but O(n²) for LinkedList.

# title: Find the frequency of each character in a string using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `text.codePoints().mapToObj(Character::toString).collect(groupingBy(identity(), counting()))`. Time: O(n). Space: O(k). Using code points avoids splitting supplementary Unicode characters.

# title: Group employees by department using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `employees.stream().collect(Collectors.groupingBy(Employee::getDepartment))`. It returns a map from department to employees. Time: O(n); space: O(n). Decide how null departments should be represented.

# title: Find the highest-paid employee in each department.
answer (Markdown): Use `groupingBy(Employee::getDepartment, maxBy(comparing(Employee::getSalary)))`. Values are Optionals because a downstream group may conceptually be empty. Time: O(n); space: O(d), where d is departments.

# title: Count employees in each department.
answer (Markdown): Use `employees.stream().collect(groupingBy(Employee::getDepartment, counting()))`. Values are `Long`. Time: O(n); space: O(d). Use a sorted map supplier if deterministic department ordering is required.

# title: Find the average employee salary using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `employees.stream().mapToDouble(Employee::getSalary).average()`. It returns `OptionalDouble`. Time: O(n); space: O(1). Consider `BigDecimal` instead of double for exact financial calculations.

# title: Find the top three highest numbers in a list.
answer (Markdown): Use `numbers.stream().sorted(Comparator.reverseOrder()).limit(3).toList()`. Time: O(n log n); space: O(n). Add `distinct()` if the requirement means three distinct values. A bounded heap is better for very large inputs.

# title: Find the top three lowest numbers in a list.
answer (Markdown): Use `numbers.stream().sorted().limit(3).toList()`. Time: O(n log n); space: O(n). Add `distinct()` for distinct values. A size-three max heap reduces large-input work to O(n log 3).

# title: Skip the first five elements and return the rest.
answer (Markdown): Use `values.stream().skip(5).toList()`. Time: O(n) to materialize the result; space: O(max(0,n-5)). If the stream contains fewer than five elements, the result is empty.

# title: Limit a list to its first five elements.
answer (Markdown): Use `values.stream().limit(5).toList()`. It is short-circuiting and returns all elements when fewer than five exist. The result preserves encounter order.

# title: Convert integers to their squares using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `numbers.stream().map(n -> n * n).toList()` or `mapToInt(n -> n * n)`. Time: O(n); space: O(n) for a collected result. Use long arithmetic if integer overflow is possible.

# title: Remove null values from a list using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `values.stream().filter(Objects::nonNull).toList()`. Time: O(n); space: O(number of non-null values). The source list remains unchanged.

# title: Concatenate two streams into one.
answer (Markdown): Use `Stream.concat(first, second)`. The resulting stream is lazy and must be consumed once; consuming it closes both inputs. Avoid reusing either source stream afterward.

# title: Generate ten random numbers using Stream API.
answer (Markdown): Use `new Random().ints(10, min, max).boxed().toList()` or `ThreadLocalRandom.current().ints(10,min,max)`. The upper bound is exclusive. Use a seeded Random when tests require repeatable values.

# title: Check whether all list elements are even.
answer (Markdown): Use `numbers.stream().allMatch(n -> n % 2 == 0)`. It short-circuits at the first odd value. For an empty stream it returns true by vacuous truth, which should be considered in business rules.

# title: Check whether any list element is odd.
answer (Markdown): Use `numbers.stream().anyMatch(n -> n % 2 != 0)`. It short-circuits after the first odd number. An empty stream returns false.

# title: Count the total elements in a list using Stream.
answer (Markdown): Use `values.stream().count()`, which returns long. For an existing Collection, `values.size()` is simpler and usually O(1); use `count()` when the source is a stream pipeline.

# title: Find the average of a list of integers.
answer (Markdown): Use `numbers.stream().mapToInt(Integer::intValue).average()`. It returns `OptionalDouble`; handle an empty list explicitly. Time: O(n); space: O(1).

# title: Find the product of all list elements.
answer (Markdown): Use `numbers.stream().reduce(1, (a,b) -> a*b)`. Time: O(n); space: O(1). The identity makes an empty stream return 1. Use long or BigInteger when overflow matters.

# title: Find palindrome strings in a list.
answer (Markdown): Use `words.stream().filter(w -> w.contentEquals(new StringBuilder(w).reverse())).toList()`. Time: O(total characters); additional space is proportional to the longest word. Normalize case if matching should be case-insensitive.

# title: Group anagrams from a list of words.
answer (Markdown): Group by a canonical key: lowercase each word, sort its characters, and collect with `groupingBy(keyFunction)`. Time: O(total k log k) for word lengths k; space: O(total input). Define case and Unicode normalization rules.

# title: Filter prime numbers from a list.
answer (Markdown): Use `numbers.stream().filter(PrimeChecker::isPrime).toList()`. `isPrime` rejects n<2 and tests divisors through `sqrt(n)`, preferably only up to `d <= n/d`. Time: O(n√m) worst case for n values bounded by m.

# title: Find numbers greater than a threshold.
answer (Markdown): Use `numbers.stream().filter(n -> n > threshold).toList()`. Time: O(n); output space: O(k). Clarify whether the boundary should be strictly greater than or greater than or equal.

# title: Map employees to their names.
answer (Markdown): Use `employees.stream().map(Employee::getName).toList()`. Time: O(n); output space: O(n). Add `distinct()` only when duplicate employee names should be removed.

# title: Create a map from employee ID to employee name.
answer (Markdown): Use `employees.stream().collect(toMap(Employee::getId, Employee::getName))`. Duplicate IDs throw an exception; provide a merge function such as `(first, second) -> first` when a duplicate policy is defined.

# title: Group transactions by type and sum their amounts.
answer (Markdown): For integer amounts use `groupingBy(Transaction::getType, summingLong(Transaction::getAmount))`. For money represented by BigDecimal, use `groupingBy(type, reducing(BigDecimal.ZERO, amount, BigDecimal::add))`. Time: O(n); space: O(types).

# title: Parallelize a stream and demonstrate parallel processing.
answer (Markdown): Use `values.parallelStream().map(this::cpuBoundOperation).toList()`. Parallel streams use the common ForkJoinPool; order-sensitive operations and shared mutable state can remove benefits or cause bugs. Benchmark realistic CPU-bound workloads before choosing parallel execution.
